Sustainability in Endoscopic Medical Congresses and Courses
  • Source: ESGE & ESGENA Position Statement, Endoscopy Journal (2026)
  • Topic: Environmental sustainability in gastrointestinal endoscopy conferences and educational events
  • Author(s): Mathieu Pioche, João A. Cunha Neves, Luca Elli, Lars Aabakken, et al. (23 members total)
1. Introduction & Background
  • Medical conferences are resource-intensive events with substantial greenhouse gas emissions
  • Primary drivers: participant travel, venue energy consumption, catering, conference materials, industry exhibitions
  • Conferences often represent the single largest contributor to carbon footprint of scientific societies
  • GI endoscopy societies face additional challenges due to live endoscopy sessions, hands-on training, animal-based courses
  • Simple reduction in educational efforts is neither feasible nor desirable
  • Challenge: rethink event design, organization, and delivery to maximize educational value while minimizing environmental harm
2. Methods Overview
  • Developed following ESGE Publications Policy (2020)
  • Position Statement format chosen due to anticipated lack of high-quality evidence
  • International multidisciplinary panel of 23 experts selected
  • Five taskforces organized to address specific topics
  • PICO framework used for structured question development
  • Systematic literature search conducted in minimum two databases through June 2025
  • GRADE system not used due to anticipated evidence limitations
  • Delphi consensus process with two voting rounds (July-August 2025, October 2025)
  • Consensus defined as ≥80% agreement (Agree + Strongly agree on 5-point Likert scale)
  • All statements reviewed and approved by ESGE Executive Committee
3. Environmental Impact of GI Endoscopy Congresses
  • Systematic search identified 17 studies assessing environmental impact of medical conferences
  • Studies were predominantly retrospective with heterogeneous methodologies
  • No conference focused on GI endoscopy has assessed its environmental impact to date
3.1 Main Emission Sources
  • Air travel contributes approximately 85%–99% of total greenhouse gas emissions
  • Per-attendee contributions vary considerably based on location and conference format
  • Long-haul flights responsible for highest emissions per attendee
  • Business class travel results in up to three times the emissions vs. economy class
  • Canadian Association of Gastroenterology annual meeting experienced a 41% increase in emissions due to longer average flight distances
  • Remote venues generate nearly twice the emissions of centrally located alternatives
3.2 Other Contributing Factors
  • Accommodation at destination contributes to environmental burden (lesser extent)
  • Venue energy use, heating, waste generation from conference materials/giveaways are relatively minor contributors
  • Canadian Association of Gastroenterology meeting: 41% increase in emissions primarily due to longer average flight distances
3.3 Conference Format Comparisons
  • Fully virtual conferences can reduce emissions by 86%–99% compared to in-person meetings
  • Hybrid models (virtual participation + regional hubs) have achieved emissions reductions of 54%–71%
  • With 25% virtual attendees → carbon footprint drops approximately 13%
  • With 75% virtual attendees → emissions reduction up to 87%
  • Emissions from interhub travel can offset some sustainability gains in hybrid formats
4. Sustainability in Event Conception & Scientific Program
4.1 Core Principles
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend establishing a "Green Plan" when organizing GI endoscopy congress or course
  • Green Plan should incorporate the "5Rs" framework:
  • Reduce
  • Reuse
  • Recycle
  • Rethink
  • Research
  • Conferences should set an example of sustainable practices
  • Demonstrates to participants how small efforts lead to significant changes
  • Promotes organizational practices consistent with European environmental commitments
STATEMENT 1 (100% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend the establishment of a "Green Plan" when starting to organize a GI endoscopy congress or course, taking the "5Rs" into consideration
STATEMENT 2 (100% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA support the educational role of medical conferences on environmental sustainability
4.2 Dedicated Sustainability Sessions
  • Major European gastroenterology conferences have allocated more space to green sessions than in the past
  • Key outcome: scientists with shared interest in sustainability have been brought closer together
  • Enables formation of working groups focused on environmental sustainability
  • Results in national and international position papers
  • Growing body of literature on "green endoscopy" promoting more sustainable practices
STATEMENT 3 (92% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend dedicated green sessions during conferences to increase sustainability awareness and create common interest groups
4.3 Live Endoscopy Events & Hands-on Training
  • ESGE Green Endoscopy Working Group survey: lack of knowledge from staff considered one of main barriers to sustainable endoscopy
  • By educating healthcare professionals (especially those in training) on sustainable practices → adoption of new technologies that are effective and environmentally conscious
  • Sustainability education should include:
  • Indication discussion of non-invasive alternatives
  • Combination of examinations
  • Choice of medical devices
  • Greener sterilization techniques
  • Waste management strategies
STATEMENT 4 (92% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend that sustainability awareness should be included during hands-on training courses and live endoscopy events
STATEMENT 5 (83% agreement)
  • When practical hands-on training is included, ESGE and ESGENA recommend transitioning to non-live-animal training models except when a clear educational benefit has been demonstrated
4.4 Non-Animal Training Model Evidence
  • Modern non-animal training models proven effective for teaching basic and advanced GI endoscopy techniques
  • Effective models include:
  • Mechanical simulators
  • Ex vivo phantoms
  • Virtual reality training
  • Live animal experimentation associated with:
  • Significant resource use
  • Pollution
  • Energy consumption
  • Waste generation
  • Impacts on biodiversity
  • Live animal training requires:
  • Special facilities
  • Veterinary care
  • Regulatory oversight
5. Reducing Transport-Related Emissions
5.1 Hybrid & Online Formats
  • Transport accounts for about 90% of carbon emission impact
  • Virtual learning significantly reduces transport burden
  • Digital sobriety measures (optimizing streaming quality, minimizing non-essential data transmission) may lower virtual participation carbon footprint by as much as 80%
STATEMENT 6 (100% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend that virtual and hybrid conference models should be encouraged
5.2 Low-Emission Travel Options
  • Switching to train travel or providing remote participation options (hybrid/virtual) can reduce carbon footprint by 78%–97%
  • Recommended strategies:
  • Reduced fees for sustainable travel
  • Train ticket support programs
  • Public transport access provision
  • Carbon footprint calculators for participants
STATEMENT 7 (96% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend that conference organizers identify and promote climate-friendly travel alternatives to attendees
STATEMENT 8 (89% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend the use of less carbon-intensive modes of transport to reach the congress facility when in-person meetings are justified
5.3 Selection of Host City
  • Choice of location determines delegate transport emissions (especially air travel)
  • Choosing geographically optimal host city can reduce emissions by nearly 20%
  • Prioritizing centrally located and easily accessible venues significantly reduces overall travel-related emissions
  • Income levels are most significant predictors of a city's carbon footprint
Recommended Assessment Tools
  • Gridded Global Model of City Footprints
  • Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP) ratings → provides insight into local waste and water management
Hub-and-Spoke Framework
  • Decentralizing attendance across regional hubs substantially reduces need for long-haul travel
  • Satellite meeting formats:
  • Limited number of speakers travel long distances
  • Most participants attend locally
  • Available data suggest emissions reduction up to 38%
Challenges for Satellite Meetings
  • Complex organizational and technical coordination required
  • Requires reliable audiovisual infrastructure
  • Synchronization across time zones needed
  • Consistent educational quality across sites must be maintained
  • Local logistics, costs, and carbon footprint of operating multiple venues must be carefully considered
STATEMENT 9 (96% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend that conference sites be carefully selected to minimize emissions, giving preference to locations with robust public transport and climate-friendly travel options
STATEMENT 10 (96% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend prioritizing low-carbon cities as host locations for congresses, with optional use of tools such as the Gridded Global Model of City Footprints or the Carbon Disclosure Project ratings to support this assessment
STATEMENT 11 (92% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA suggest considering decentralized satellite meetings in multiple selected locations as they can minimize travel-related emissions
5.4 Selection of Faculty
  • Scientific and educational excellence must remain primary criteria for selecting external speakers
  • Must respect equity and inclusivity principles
  • These criteria must be balanced against environmental cost of travel
  • Strategies to reduce faculty carbon footprint:
  • Favoring local experts
  • Prioritizing speakers who can travel by train
  • Offering virtual participation to distant faculty
STATEMENT 12 (87% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend adapting faculty selection to the conference location (e.g., giving preference to local experts or those able to travel by train). The carbon footprint of faculty attendance should be considered alongside scientific and educational merit
6. Venue & Accommodation
6.1 Selection of Venue
  • Selecting venue with recognized sustainability policy or certification significantly reduces environmental impact
  • Recommended certifications:
  • Green Key (global eco-certification for hotels and venues)
  • Compliance with international standard ISO 20121
  • Certified venues often:
  • Promote sustainable procurement
  • Encourage reuse of materials (signage, marketing stands)
  • Avoid single-use plastics
  • Support low-impact catering and resource-efficient practices
STATEMENT 13 (96% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend favoring venues with established sustainability policies and certifications, ensuring that environmental criteria are part of the venue selection process
6.2 Onsite Actions
  • "5Rs" framework for green endoscopy: recycling + rethink, reduce, reuse, research
  • Introduction of easily comprehensible waste segregation and recycling system recommended
  • These measures reduce CO₂ emissions from waste incineration and landfill
  • Delegates should receive training on waste separation
STATEMENT 14 (100% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend the use of appropriate onsite recycling bins to help reduce the waste sent to landfills
STATEMENT 15 (93% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend that brochures or conference schedules should be provided in digital format or, if necessary, in products made from recycled, biodegradable, and sustainable materials
6.3 Catering
  • Catering represents significant component of carbon footprint
  • Accounts for approximately 16%–30% of all non-travel CO₂ emissions
  • Major contributors: food production, transport, and consumption
  • Animal-based products (particularly red meat and fish) are major contributors to emissions
Emission Reduction by Menu Type
  • Replacing standard menus with vegetarian options → reduces catering-related emissions by around 30%
  • Fully vegan menus may achieve reductions of up to 60%
STATEMENT 16 (92% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend vegetarian event catering whenever possible to reduce environmental burden
STATEMENT 17 (96% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend favoring locally farmed, seasonal, and fresh food, and using reusable dishes, cups, and cutlery
6.4 Accommodation
  • Hotel overnight stays contribute 4%–15% of CO₂ emissions from medical congresses
  • Emissions are location-dependent:
Location kgCO₂e per room-night
France 7.2
Spain 17.6
US 36.2
  • Emissions depend on sustainability policies of hotels
  • To reduce hotel-related emissions:
  • Select accommodations with sustainable measures
  • Available eco-certificates for hotels provide guidance
STATEMENT 18 (86% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend the prioritization of accommodation with certified sustainable policies
STATEMENT 19 (96% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend the selection of accommodations with good access to the conference venue (i.e., via public transport or within walking distance)
7. Benchmarking Green Performance
7.1 Assessing Environmental Impact
  • No published studies analyzing benefits, costs, or harms of measuring carbon footprint of scientific conferences found
  • Rationale for implementing assessments is compelling because they:
  • Facilitate informed decision-making
  • Demonstrate accountability
  • Support a culture of sustainability
  • Integrating environmental impact assessments into conference planning can drive meaningful change
  • Tracking metrics provides clear benchmarks for sustainable improvement:
  • Carbon emissions
  • Energy consumption
  • Waste production
Challenges Identified
  • Lack of standardized methodologies
  • Need for dedicated resources
  • Potential organizational burdens (especially for international, multi-institutional events)
STATEMENT 20 (100% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA suggest considering that GI endoscopy congresses and courses assess their environmental impact
Post-Event Sustainability Reports
  • Producing post-event sustainability report is increasingly regarded as best practice for scientific societies and conference organizers
  • Benefits:
  • Allows benchmarking of performance
  • Identifies strengths and gaps
  • Sets measurable targets for subsequent editions
  • Builds credibility and accountability through transparency
  • Demonstrates society's commitment to tracking real-world footprint and continuous improvement
STATEMENT 21 (96% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend producing a sustainability report following each event, highlighting areas for improvement for the next edition
7.2 Carbon Offsetting
  • Carbon offsetting should be implemented alongside direct reduction strategies
  • Unavoidable emissions can be neutralized through verified carbon credits (e.g., Gold Standard)
  • Offsetting should occur after all feasible reductions have been implemented:
  • Virtual participation
  • Hybrid formats
  • Regional satellite sessions
Policy Context
  • European Green Deal aims for:
  • At least 55% emissions reduction by 2030
  • Climate neutrality by 2050
  • Embedding event-level sustainability into broader policy frameworks
Criticisms & Limitations
  • Offsets do not yield immediate benefits (many projects deliver avoided emissions years later)
  • Risk of greenwashing
  • Over-reliance may distract from systemic solutions (e.g., reduced flying, improved logistics)
  • Offsetting should never replace rigorous emissions reduction at source
STATEMENT 22 (96% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend that GI endoscopy congresses and courses implement direct emission reduction strategies or carbon-offsetting initiatives to minimize the environmental impact and promote sustainability
8. Recommendations for Industry Partners
  • By favoring low-carbon transport (e.g., train instead of flights) or virtual attendance for some staff → industry partners can drastically lower travel-related emissions
  • Promotional materials, giveaways ("goodies"), packaging, single-use plastics linked to industry booths or sponsorship contribute significantly to event waste burden
STATEMENT 23 (100% agreement)
  • ESGE and ESGENA recommend that industry partners supporting meetings adopt low-waste and low-carbon practices
9. Key Statistics Summary
Emission Sources at Medical Conferences
Source Contribution
Participant travel 85%–99%
Venue & energy use Minor
Accommodation 4%–15%
Food & catering 16%–30% (non-travel)
Conference Format Impact
Format/Measure Emissions Reduction
Fully virtual vs. in-person 86%–99%
Hybrid models 54%–71%
25% virtual attendees ~13% reduction
75% virtual attendees ~87% reduction
Train travel vs. flying 78%–97%
Satellite meetings up to 38%
Catering Impact
Menu Type Emissions Reduction
Vegetarian vs. standard ~30% reduction
Fully vegan menus up to 60% reduction
Accommodation Emissions (per room-night)
  • France: 7.2 kgCO₂e
  • Spain: 17.6 kgCO₂e
  • US: 36.2 kgCO₂e
10. Final Statements Summary Table
Statement Topic Agreement
1 Green Plan with 5Rs framework 100%
2 Educational role on sustainability 100%
3 Dedicated green sessions at conferences 92%
4 Sustainability in hands-on training & live events 92%
5 Transition to non-live-animal models when possible 83%
6 Encourage virtual and hybrid formats 100%
7 Promote climate-friendly travel alternatives 96%
8 Use less carbon-intensive transport to venue 89%
9 Careful site selection with public transport access 96%
10 Prioritize low-carbon cities using assessment tools 96%
11 Consider decentralized satellite meetings 92%
12 Adapt faculty selection to location/transport mode 87%
13 Favor venues with sustainability certifications 96%
14 Use onsite recycling bins 100%
15 Digital or sustainable printed materials 93%
16 Vegetarian catering when possible 92%
17 Local, seasonal food; reusable dishes/cutlery 96%
18 Prioritize certified sustainable accommodation 86%
19 Select accommodations near venue (walking/transit) 96%
20 Assess environmental impact 100%
21 Produce sustainability report post-event 96%
22 Implement reduction strategies or carbon offsets 96%
23 Industry partners adopt low-waste/low-carbon practices 100%
11. Conclusions
  • Medical conferences are fundamental to progress in GI endoscopy (innovation, training, international collaboration)
  • Traditional conference models associated with substantial environmental burden
  • Environmental impact driven predominantly by travel-related emissions, also by venue operations, catering, accommodation, resource consumption
  • Improving sustainability of professional education has become an ethical responsibility for scientific societies
Key Implementation Strategies
  • Promote virtual and hybrid formats
  • Optimize conference location and accessibility
  • Encourage low-carbon travel options
  • Adapt faculty selection based on transport mode
  • Prioritize sustainable venues and accommodation
  • Reduce waste and printed materials
  • Improve catering practices (vegetarian/vegan options)
  • Favor non-animal training models when educationally appropriate
  • Systematic assessment and transparent reporting of environmental impact
Future Commitment
  • ESGE and ESGENA will engage to progressively implement these recommendations in their own congresses, courses, and endorsed events
  • Goal: deliver high-quality education while contributing to more sustainable healthcare and scientific practice
  • Shared responsibility among organizers, faculty, industry partners, and participants
